questions: Locate the specific tier of the U.S. federal judiciary defined as the 13 courts sitting immediately below the Supreme Court. Provide the official name of this court tier, the standard number of judges on its decision-making panels, and the jurisdictional distinction between the 12 regional circuits and the single 'Federal Circuit'. Additionally, referencing federal supervision standards, identify the specific three-part model used to guide officer assessment and the two acronym-based tools used to predict risk for pretrial and post-conviction individuals.

rounds.question: Locate the specific tier of the U.S. federal judiciary defined as the 13 courts sitting immediately below the Supreme Court. Provide the official name of this court tier, the standard number of judges on its decision-making panels, and the jurisdictional distinction between the 12 regional circuits and the single 'Federal Circuit'. Additionally, referencing federal supervision standards, identify the specific three-part model used to guide officer assessment and the two acronym-based tools used to predict risk for pretrial and post-conviction individuals.
checklist_width: Court Tier Name: U.S. Courts of Appeals (or Circuit Courts)
Panel Composition: Panels of three judges
Regional Circuits Jurisdiction: Geographic/Review cases within specific circuits
Federal Circuit Jurisdiction: Nationwide jurisdiction (specialized cases/patents)
Three Principles: Risk Principle, Need Principle, Responsivity Principle
Post-Conviction Tool: PCRA (Post Conviction Risk Assessment)
checklist_depth: Target Entity 1: U.S. Courts of Appeals (Logic: Identified via '13 courts sitting immediately below the Supreme Court')
Target Entity 2: Risk-Need-Responsivity Model & Tools (Logic: Identified via 'Evidence-Based Practices' and supervision context)
rounds.rationale: The question employs Deep Logic by describing the court tier through its hierarchical position (13 courts below Supreme Court) rather than naming it directly. It utilizes Wide Aggregation by requiring the agent to synthesize structural judicial details (panels, jurisdiction types) with distinct probation methodologies (RNR model, PTRA/PCRA tools) located in separate sections of the source text.
source_nodes: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Evidence-Based Practices Probation and pretrial services officers are committed to using evidence-based practices (EBP) to promote public safety and positively impact the live
rounds.answer_a:
The tier of the U.S. federal judiciary immediately below the Supreme Court consists of the United States Courts of Appeals, also known as the Circuit Courts [1]. There are 13 appellate courts: 12 regional circuits and one specialized court. Cases are typically heard by rotating three-judge panels selected from the pool of judges within each circuit [2]. The 12 regional circuits have jurisdiction over federal appeals originating within their geographic regions, handling diverse federal legal issues, including constitutional challenges and federal statutes [3]. In contrast, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit has nationwide jurisdiction limited to specialized subject areas such as patents, international trade, government contracts, and certain money claims against the federal government [4].

Regarding federal supervision standards, the U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services System employs the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model to guide officer assessments of individuals under supervision [5]. This three-part framework evaluates an individual’s risk of reoffending (Risk), identifies criminogenic needs (Need), and tailors interventions based on learning styles and personal circumstances (Responsivity). To support risk prediction, two evidence-based tools are utilized: the Pretrial Risk Assessment Tool (PRA) for individuals released before trial [6], and the Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) for those under community supervision following conviction [7]. These tools enhance consistency and objectivity in supervision decisions across federal districts.

## References  
[1] United States Courts - About the Federal Courts  
[2] Federal Judicial Center - Organization of the U.S. Court of Appeals  
[3] Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute - U.S. Federal Courts  
[4] U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit - Jurisdiction  
[5] National Institute of Justice - Risk-Need-Responsivity Model  
[6] Bureau of Justice Assistance - Pretrial Risk Assessment Tool  
[7] U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services - Risk Assessment Instruments
